sentences.text: By this time, my grandfather was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very sick.
Because, though we don't not told him, he know we are not telling him the truth.
And he just found out this himself, or he was told by other Jews.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was very weak, but he was carrying on and hoping that she come home.
They keep my father in jail almost until they took us to Auschwitz.
He was never allowed to come home.
And he was taken to the barracks when all the Jews from there-- all the Jews was taken to Auschwitz.
And he was there for five days.
And after this, we taken to Auschwitz.
Both of my brothers was in soldiers' camps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One was taken out from the country, probably in Ukraine.
He's missing.
This was Charles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My other brother was also in soldiers' camp, though he run away.
He ran to Budapest.
But he was catched and taken back.
He lived through the life-- the war.
And he emigrated to Israel.
We was allowed to stay home for another-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three weeks.
And after, we have to give up our home.
We was got one day to get ready for this, to moving some of our things.
And this was the time when they took all the Jews to another place, which was called the Sighet.
This is, again, the island.
They gathered the Jews there to get at the one who was home.
And from there, we was taken to the barracks.
It was 1944 in April.
17, between 18.
Yes.
I remember this day.
We did find a mover who took us our bed, or something like this, mother and me.
But we was ready for everything.
And we did have one-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my brother's wife, the one who was taken to Ukraine, was living in the Sighet all the time.
So we went and moved with her family.
And there, was we staying maybe five days before we was taken to the barracks.
My sisters was married.
One was in Sopron, and the other one was hiding in Budapest.
There where the ghetto was, yes.
It wasn't a ghetto.
It was really private houses, because most of the Jews lived there.
And the one whose home was taken usually find someone who get them into their own houses, or you just stay with them.
The condition was there very crowded.
Because, say, when we was moving into my brother's wife, they did not have a large home.
Usually they have maybe-- what you got, a room and a kitchen.
Because European life is different.
And this all she have, with one child.
She have a child.
My grandfather still at his house, the same place, in Sighet, very close to us.
He insist to waiting for grandmother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, he was not too far, a few houses farther than where we were.
My mother's state of mind was very, very good.
She want me to hide, too.
She was very happy that one of my sisters is hiding.
One of my father's workers offered her, and to me, to hiding me.
And she really want me to go with this, but I decided I'm not going with her.
Because I wouldn't leave my mother alone anymore.
I was ready for this.
Plus, I was scared, also, to go with someone I hardly know.
But mother know him.
He was a student in my father's workshop.
I can't remember his name.
But coincidentally, he was a soldier also in the jail, where my father was staying.
And at night, when nobody see it, he come and gave us messages from father.
He was really risking his own life to do this.
About five days.
That's what I was like to point out, which is a very hard thing for all the women endure, which was happening.
Because before, we was taken to the barracks-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they wanted everything you have in gold-- everything.
So they take you to one place, and they said, you still have gold, and you did not give it to us.
There was about-- man in one room and a woman in the other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First they looked through mother.
They didn't find anything in her, because we wouldn't do things like this.
And then I have to go through with this.
And it was a very torturous things to do.
And I never forgot my mother screaming and pain when it was happen with me, because I was roughly, roughly treated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's happened with most of the young girls.
If they could, they'd destroy you, or half destroy you, so you never be the same woman again.
Because even if you were hurt, there was no doctor, there was no help.
And they was very disappointed if they don't find this gold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But before we went into this, we have some gold in our necklace, some ring and things.
We gave them-- this to them, but this wasn't enough.
And they was really known by the Hungarian government and everybody.
They was Hungarian.
But they was worse than the German.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I know one of the person, but I just know whom he was.
Very badly.
Very badly.
My hurt-- not as much-- I was badly hurt, but to seeing the first time my mother suffered through was bad.
[DRINKS]
Well, after this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was over--
We couldn't go nowhere.
We have to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go back where we was lived in my brother's wife's house.
I don't remember.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember whether we talked, because we still hardly, hardly gone home, but we wasn't too far from where we lived.
This happened in Sighet.
So we went home, and my poor mother put me in bed and tried to do whatever she can.
And we was another five days in this place when they gathering together about three, four transfers of Jews, say maybe 200, 300, one of a time.
And then they took you to the barracks.
What were the barracks?
Where were they?
The barracks was in a highway, usually.
This was meant for soldiers, used to live there, like wooden building.
You was allowed to take some parcels and clothes with you, like blankets and things.
And we have to sleep in the floors.
We got some food there for about another five, six days before we was taken to the wagons to go to Auschwitz.
No-- nothing.
Because anything we took to Auschwitz, it was taken from us anyway.
And when you arrived to Auschwitz, they undress you, are naked, cutting your hair.
And either you go to one side to live, either you go to the other side, which we did not know.
They are one side for life, the other side maybe have a chance to survive.
Yes.
By then, my grandfather wasn't able to walk.
Because the barracks from the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the Sighet was a long way.
So he had to have some transportation.
By then, he got-- his eyes got very bad.
His walking got very bad.
He was very, very sick.
So we couldn't comfort him, because we don't have comfort ourselves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- he was taken by transfer to the barrack.
My father was-- all the men who was in the jail was taken to the barracks right from the jail.
It was probably, I would think, maybe 50 or something like close.
There we met our father again, like, we were together, mother, father, and us, but not with our grandfather.
And grandmother, yes.
We was taken by closed wagons.
I wouldn't exactly know how many in one wagon-- maybe 80 to 100.
But I don't know exactly the numbers.
And it was very, very closed and hot in these wagons.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother felt sick.
She was having this kind of heat waves.
I wasn't too bad myself.
Neither was my father as bad as mother.
Mother was sick.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I remember right, it was taken six to seven days till we reach Auschwitz.
Well, it was kind of barrack like of some building, and sign-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what kind of a building where they are.
There was a Jewish person come and help us to throw out our parcels.
And then there was the German soldiers, who was-- tell you, you go to the right, you go to the left.
Mother goes here.
And the men was, again, has to go on their own direction.
And that's how we were separated.
We could say a few words to each other.
My mother asked me not to say no to the Germans, because she don't want I'll be beaten again, like some of the other person was who wasn't separated.
My mother asked me, do not do this.
Because she just don't feel like this should happen again.
My father say goodbye to me.
He also said, probably, you might stay in life.
You have a chance.
Hold on, whatever happen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I think the other one-- all of us would be killed.
He knew it in a moment he stepped foot in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after this, we was taken again to a barrack-- at least I was.
I don't see Mom or Dad anymore.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We was about 1,000 in the barrack.
I was taken after they cut my hair and take all my clothing and just gave me one rag to cover myself.
This was all-- nothing else.
After we went in a barrack, there was Polish girls, who was there for five years.
They told us we are very lucky, because they have been in here in five days, and they told us everything they could.
Well, they told you, you are in a death camp.
You might have a chance to go to work.
But this is not for sure.
And the kind of food and the kind of life we have to live there.
One sleep-- we sleep on the floor again.
One has their head down one way.
The other, you know, had their foot.
And every morning, there was an appell.
When the German come, they counting us.
We have to stay five in line.
And there were quite few mother who was let stay in life, but their children had been taken from them.
And they was very, very sick after their children's-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About two or three weeks later, I was taken back to another place from this barrack, to a second barrack.
And maybe with 50 numbers on this barrack, we was again looked over by the Germans.
What happened with the rest, I do not know, but about 15 or so was taken back to the barracks where I was taken.
This was Mengele, who was again selected the girls.
And I was taken back.
And again, everybody questioned, what happened?
What happened with the others?
But we had no answer.
The only thing-- they just don't believe they send us back, and they did not know for why.
It's happened one more times-- same thing.
And the third times happened again.
This time, I was sent to Reichenbach for a working place.
Six weeks.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe the fifth days and about five to six weeks-- more six weeks than five weeks, when I was taken from Auschwitz this time.
If they won't take me this time, I wouldn't last much longer in Auschwitz.
Because I was sick, and when I went to, you know, to the washroom, blood start to come from my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stomach.
And this was pretty well-- if it's starting, you'll be taken to the crematorium anyway.
Because how long could you hold on?
It was my nerve-- loneliness, sadness, the food and the cold-- where you sleep at night, which was cold, and daytime, the sun was killing you.
It was really hot at daytime.
And I don't really know what more.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Whenever you have any illness, you just kept quiet, because if you tell them or if they know it themselves, you look sick, that's the end of your life.
And I wanted to live through, to hoping just to live through.
Because my father also told us, when he saw me the last, it couldn't last too long, maybe another four weeks.
And you might be able to hold on.
He was wrong.
It was lasted another year.
Reichenbach is a smaller city, I think, was somewhere near Poland, or was maybe in Poland, more like Poland than Germany.
It was a city where all the prisoners working, many, many thousands of them in different camps they was living.
The most famous was the [INAUDIBLE].
And it was about three, four kilometers from the place I used to work.
But where I live, it was almost about a 3/4 hour's walk only from the factory.
A radio factory.
I was making lamps for radios or for boats.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, no.
There was different kind of factories, many of them.
I only knew the one I was working, because you're coming, talking with the other prisoners.
Some was working fieldwork.
And-- and in different factories, but they was all manufacturing for the war something, except the field workers.

Where I was living it was the best condition, I was told, whatever they gave to the prisoners, because this was a special lager where they even brought in the Red Cross sometimes, to show how the prisoners lived.
But this place was only maybe 100 Jewish prisoners and maybe 60 Dutch prisoners.
All the others didn't have the way we was living, because we have our bunk bed with straw and a blanket.
And we also able to wash ourselves daily.
Yes.
